title: Federal Judge Issues Injunction Restricting ICE Use of Force Against Minneapolis Protesters
excerpt: U.S. District Judge Katherine Menendez issued a temporary injunction barring ICE and other federal agents from retaliating against peaceful protesters in Minneapolis and requiring reasonable articulable suspicion before stopping vehicles, following documented abuses during Operation Metro Surge.
content:
On January 16, 2026, U.S. District Judge Katherine Menendez issued a temporary injunction in Minneapolis restricting the use of force by ICE and other federal officers following widespread allegations of abuse during Operation Metro Surge.

The ruling prohibited ICE agents from retaliating against peaceful and unobstructive protesters and required agents to have reasonable articulable suspicion of a federal offense before stopping a vehicle. Testimony presented included at least 23 instances in which the court found individuals were stopped without "reasonable, articulable suspicion" during the operation, including U.S. citizens and lawful residents who were stopped based on race or ethnicity.

  • A separate federal ruling by Judge Menendez later found ICE had likely made "unconstitutional" stops but declined to issue a preliminary injunction after Operation Metro Surge wound down
  • The ACLU stated the court found "a clear showing that the agents were following an unlawful policy to stop and arrest Minnesotans in violation of their Fourth Amendment rights"
  • The injunction was later stayed by a federal appellate court on the administration's emergency request
